topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice, stark white against the faded beige of the campus bulletin board, felt like a physical blow. Elara traced the outline of the closing date with a fingertip, the paper crinkling under the pressure. Her hands, usually steady when navigating crowded lecture halls, were trembling now, the fine vibrations mirroring the frantic flutter in her chest. She retreated to her dorm room, the silence of the small space amplifying the hollow echo in her ears. Every shadow seemed to stretch and deepen, turning the familiar posters of student life into mocking specters. She sat on the edge of her bed, pulling her knees to her chest, trying to compress herself into a smaller, less noticeable shape. A lump formed in her throat, a tight, unfamiliar constriction that made swallowing difficult. She stared at the framed photo of her friends laughing, the joy in their eyes feeling impossibly distant, like a dream she was waking from too soon.

[story 2]

"They're really shutting down?" Marcus whispered, the words catching in his throat as he looked at the final memo taped to the library door. He didn't move, just stood there, his shoulders slumping as if the weight of the news had suddenly become unbearable. The air in the hallway felt thick and suffocating, heavy with unspoken finality. He ran a hand over his face, feeling the heat rise in his cheeks, a flush that had nothing to do with exertion. He started pacing, a restless energy churning beneath his skin, unable to settle into any single spot. When he finally turned back to his roommate, his voice was barely above a breath, a shaky exhale. "I don't know what to do," he admitted, the admission tasting like ash. He just stood, letting the enormity of the situation wash over him, his gaze fixed on a scuff mark on the worn linoleum floor.

[story 3]

I kept re-reading the email, the words swimming before my eyes, refusing to cohere into a sensible narrative. The world outside my window seemed muted, the vibrant greens of the campus lawn dull and lifeless. A coldness settled deep in my core, a pervasive chill that no amount of blanket could dispel. I found myself curled under my duvet, hugging my knees so tightly that my knuckles turned white. Every sound—the distant rumble of a bus, the rustle of leaves—was magnified, sharp and intrusive. My breath hitched occasionally, a small, involuntary spasm in my chest. It felt as if the foundation of my entire routine, the scaffolding of my future, had simply dissolved, leaving me adrift in a vast, terrifying emptiness. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the swirling anxiety down, but it only churned harder, a relentless, nauseating tide.
